title: TPU v1 · Silicon Validation and Characterization: After bring-up makes the chip operational, real workloads must validate function and performance against specification. PVT characterization then sweeps voltage × frequency × temperature, builds pass/fail shmoo plots, measures actual maximum frequency, power, and guardband, and compares silicon with signoff predictions to confirm that 700 MHz ≈ 92 TOPS and 28–40 W are real and sufficiently margined.
oneLine: This case supports the silicon-validation and characterization section. Once bring-up in 3.1–3.3 has activated the chip and established basic operation, two harder questions remain: does it really meet specification, and how much margin remains across PVT? ① Silicon validation executes real neural-network inference—not merely activation instructions—to confirm correctness and performance. The paper targets 700 MHz ≈ 92 TOPS and a 24 MiB Unified Buffer able to feed the systolic array; those commitments must be demonstrated on real silicon under real workloads. ② Characterization tests more than one nominal point. Across PVT—P for fast/typical/slow process corner, V for high/nominal/low voltage, and T for low/room/high temperature—it sweeps voltage × frequency × temperature to measure the actual maximum stable frequency, actual power, and guardband to failure. A classic deliverable is the shmoo plot: each voltage/frequency grid point runs a test and is colored green for pass or red for fail, showing whether the 700 MHz nominal-voltage specification point sits safely inside the pass region. ③ Silicon-versus-signoff correlation compares measured frequency, power, and margin with STA and power-analysis predictions. Agreement validates the signoff models; a large discrepancy triggers investigation of signoff models/libraries or process/silicon. This is standard practice for a 28 nm-class PCIe accelerator. Known TPU attributes—the 256×256 array at ≈92 TOPS, 700 MHz, 24 MiB UB, 28 nm, 28–40 W, and PCIe card—are only the subject of this methodology. TPU v1's private shmoo plots and PVT measurements are not public.

TPU v1's private plots and PVT frequency/power/margin are not public; known 700 MHz/92 TOPS/24 MiB UB/28–40 W/PCIe specifications are only the example. Grid data are illustrative.</text></svg>

legend.name: ① Silicon validation (confirm function and performance in real scenarios)
legend.desc: Bring-up proves that a chip powers, responds, and can execute basic operations. Silicon validation proves that it actually meets specification under a representative workload. On TPU v1 this means real neural-network inference driven by the host's roughly dozen CISC instructions and the TensorFlow/XLA stack, with correctness, throughput, and latency checked end to end. Paper claims such as ~92 TOPS from the array at 700 MHz and a 24 MiB Unified Buffer able to sustain it must be demonstrated on real silicon, not inferred from simulation or a few activation vectors. The exact workloads and criteria are not public.

legend.name: ② Characterization (measure actual frequency, power, and margin across PVT)
legend.desc: Validation asks whether the chip passes; characterization asks where its boundary lies. Measure more than the nominal point across PVT: fast/typical/slow process samples, high/nominal/low voltage, and low/room/high temperature. At these combinations, determine maximum stable frequency, actual power, and guardband to failure. For TPU v1, the conceptual checks are whether 700 MHz remains stable, power stays within 28–40 W, and margin survives temperature and process variation. Scan points and private measurements are not public.

legend.name: Shmoo plot (voltage × frequency grid; green pass / red fail)
legend.desc: A shmoo plot visualizes characterization. Its axes are voltage and frequency; each grid point runs the selected test and is colored green for pass, red for fail, or optionally yellow for marginal behavior. The resulting map exposes the stable voltage-frequency region and failure region. A specification point such as 700 MHz at nominal voltage should lie well inside the green region, not merely on its edge. Each fixed process/temperature corner receives its own plot. TPU v1's private shmoo plots are not public; this grid is illustrative.

legend.name: Specification point + operating guardband (dark-green cell)
legend.desc: The highlighted shmoo cell represents the target operating point—conceptually 700 MHz at nominal voltage for TPU. Passing that single cell is insufficient. Guardband is the distance to the red failure boundary, and production silicon must tolerate process variation, supply movement, temperature change, and aging. The specification point therefore needs surrounding green cells. A thin margin creates long-term risk under sustained data-center load and temperature drift. Exact margin requirements are project-specific and are not public for TPU v1.

legend.name: ③ Silicon-versus-signoff correlation (did measurements deliver the analysis?)
legend.desc: Before tapeout, signoff uses static timing analysis and power analysis to predict 700 MHz closure, approximately 28–40 W power, and available margin. Characterization compares measured frequency, power, and guardband with those predictions. Agreement shows that silicon delivered the paper numbers and that signoff models are credible. If silicon is slower, hotter, or less margined, determine whether process libraries/models were inaccurate—requiring model correction—or whether the lot/process has a real issue requiring foundry action. TPU v1's correlation data are not public.

steps.cap: ① Silicon validation: run a real scenario on the activated TPU—PCIe host CISC commands and a TensorFlow/XLA-compiled neural-network inference workload—and verify correctness, throughput, and latency. Demonstrate 700 MHz ≈ 92 TOPS and adequate 24 MiB-UB delivery on real silicon, not merely with activation vectors.

② Characterization: across PVT—fast/typical/slow process, high/nominal/low voltage, and low/room/high temperature—sweep voltage × frequency × temperature. Measure maximum stable frequency, actual power, and distance to failure at every corner to answer whether the worst case still meets specification.

③ Build and interpret shmoo plots: mark each voltage/frequency point green for pass or red for fail. The 700 MHz nominal-voltage specification point must sit safely inside the green region with guardband rather than barely touching the failure boundary.

④ Render the silicon-versus-signoff verdict: compare measured frequency, power, and margin with STA/power predictions. Agreement proves 700 MHz / 92 TOPS / 28–40 W in silicon; a large mismatch requires investigation of signoff models/libraries or the silicon process.

before: Start with stable, activated silicon from bring-up: silicon validation and characterization follow bring-up. Sections 3.1–3.3 must have produced one or more cards that power reliably, accept PCIe commands, and execute basic paths. Characterization has no meaning on a device that is not yet operational.

Define compliance criteria on both function and performance: specify which functions must be correct and which performance limits must pass—conceptually ~92 TOPS at 700 MHz, inference throughput/latency, and a 28–40 W power envelope for TPU. Without explicit limits, a running chip cannot be declared compliant. Private TPU criteria are not public.

Prepare representative validation workloads: use real neural-network inference, a TensorFlow/XLA-generated instruction stream, and the PCIe data path rather than only simple activation vectors. Representative execution exposes silicon behavior missed by isolated tests.

Plan the PVT matrix and shmoo conditions: select process samples, voltage range, temperature setpoints, voltage/frequency step sizes, and the pass/fail workload for every cell. Matrix coverage and resolution determine the credibility of characterization. TPU v1's private matrix is not public.

Obtain signoff predictions as the comparison baseline: collect signoff STA, power, frequency, and margin projections before measurement. Without the paper baseline, the team cannot determine whether real silicon delivered the design analysis.
during: The objective is simple: prove on real silicon that the paper numbers are true and margined. Bring-up proves operation; validation uses real scenarios to establish functional and performance compliance; characterization measures actual frequency, power, and guardband across PVT. The question is whether 700 MHz ≈ 92 TOPS and 28–40 W are not only possible but stable.

Validate with real scenarios, not only activation vectors. Run real neural-network inference: the host issues roughly a dozen CISC instructions over PCIe, TensorFlow/XLA generates the stream, data enters the 24 MiB Unified Buffer, and the systolic array executes INT8 MACs. Check correctness, throughput, and latency on this complete path because only real workloads expose realistic silicon interactions.

Characterize across PVT instead of testing only nominal. Manufactured parts span fast and slow process corners; voltage varies; data-center temperature spans low to high. Sweep voltage and frequency at multiple PVT corners to measure maximum stable frequency, actual power, and distance to failure. Pay special attention to slow process + low voltage + high temperature for timing and the hottest corner for power.

Use shmoo plots to require a specification point safely inside the pass region. Run a test at every voltage/frequency cell, mark pass green, fail red, and marginal yellow. The important result is not merely that the specification cell passes, but that surrounding cells pass too. A 700 MHz nominal-voltage point adjacent to red is barely compliant and may fail after production variation or aging.

Close the silicon-versus-signoff loop. Compare measured frequency, power, and guardband with STA and power-analysis predictions. Agreement validates 700 MHz / 92 TOPS / 28–40 W and the signoff models. If silicon misses frequency, consumes more power, or loses margin, distinguish inaccurate process libraries/models—then recalibrate them—from an actual silicon/process problem—then work with the foundry.

Evidence boundary: real-workload validation, PVT voltage/frequency/temperature characterization, pass/fail shmoo plots, guardband evaluation, and signoff correlation are standard practices for 28 nm-class PCIe accelerators. TPU v1's private shmoo plots, per-corner frequency/power/margin, and correlation results are not public. This case uses only the known 256×256 array at ≈92 TOPS, 700 MHz, 24 MiB UB, 28 nm, 28–40 W, and PCIe-card form; grid data are illustrative.

result.label: Deliverable
result.value: A silicon-validation and characterization report: real-workload function/performance verdict, PVT shmoo plots and measured frequency/power/margin, plus the silicon-versus-signoff correlation decision.

result.label: Silicon validation
result.value: Execute a real neural-network workload—PCIe CISC commands and TF/XLA-generated flow—on real TPU silicon and verify function, throughput, latency, and power. Demonstrate the paper's 700 MHz ≈ 92 TOPS under a real workload.

result.label: Characterization across PVT
result.value: Measure maximum stable frequency, actual power, and guardband at process fast/typical/slow × voltage high/nominal/low × temperature low/room/high corners.

result.label: Shmoo plot
result.value: Voltage × frequency grid with green pass, red fail, and yellow marginal. The 700 MHz nominal-voltage point must sit safely inside the green region with guardband, not barely on the failure edge.

result.label: Silicon-versus-signoff verdict
result.value: Measured frequency/power/margin agrees with STA and power predictions → paper numbers delivered in silicon; large mismatch → investigate inaccurate signoff models/process libraries or a real silicon/process issue.

result.label: Evidence boundary
result.value: Shmoo plots, per-PVT measurements, and silicon-signoff correlation are standard private characterization artifacts, not published TPU v1 data. Known TPU specifications serve only as the validation target.

after: Require both validation and characterization margin to pass: confirm function/performance under real scenarios and verify that the specification point in every PVT shmoo remains green with adequate guardband. Pay particular attention to slow process + low voltage + high temperature for frequency and the hottest corner for power. Any failure blocks production release.

Investigate model versus process when measurements diverge: if silicon misses frequency, consumes excess power, or loses margin relative to signoff, determine whether process libraries/models were inaccurate—then recalibrate them—or whether the lot/process has a real problem—then drive foundry correction. This closes paper prediction against silicon fact.

Feed characterization into production test and binning: measured frequency, power, and margin determine manufacturing test conditions and performance/power bins. Provide stable V/F points and weak corners so volume screening classifies devices on evidence.

Track any thin-margin corner explicitly: when a specification point approaches the red boundary, isolate whether design margin, process spread, or one critical path/module is responsible. Adjust voltage/frequency, restrict the supported PVT range, or correct design/process as appropriate. A data-center device expected to operate at 24×7 sustained load cannot ignore this reliability risk.

Preserve an auditable record: archive validation conclusions, shmoo plots, per-corner frequency/power/margin, and signoff correlations for production test, reliability, and failure-analysis teams. These measurements are standard proprietary characterization data, not public TPU v1 disclosures.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the paper discloses the 256×256 8-bit-MAC systolic array = 65,536 MACs, 700 MHz ≈ 92 TOPS (INT8), 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 28 nm / ≤331 mm² / 28–40 W implementation, roughly a dozen CISC instructions delivered over PCIe 3.0, PCIe accelerator-card form, and TensorFlow/XLA software stack. Those facts serve only as the validation and characterization target. Real-workload silicon validation, PVT process/voltage/temperature characterization, actual frequency/power/guardband measurement, pass/fail shmoo plots, and comparison against signoff STA and power analysis are standard manufacturing, packaging/test, and production practices for 28 nm-class PCIe accelerators. The shmoo grid and PVT data are illustrative, not TPU v1's private plots, measurements, or silicon-versus-signoff results.)
